title: Generative Flows on Discrete State-Spaces Enabling Multimodal Flows with Applications to Protein Co-Design
abstract: Combining discrete and continuous data is an important capability for generative models. We present Discrete Flow Models (DFMs), a new flow-based model of discrete data that provides the missing link in enabling flow-based generative models to be applied to multimodal continuous and discrete data problems. Our key insight is that the discrete equivalent of continuous space flow matching can be realized using Continuous Time Markov Chains. DFMs benefit from a simple derivation that includes discrete diffusion models as a specific instance while allowing improved performance over existing diffusion-based approaches. We utilize our DFMs method to build a multimodal flow-based modeling framework. We apply this capability to the task of protein co-design, wherein we learn a model for jointly generating protein structure and sequence. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art co-design performance while allowing the same multimodal model to be used for flexible generation of the sequence or structure.
impact: In this paper we work to advance general purpose generative modeling techniques, specifically those used for modeling discrete and multimodal data. We apply these techniques to the task of protein generation. Improving protein modeling capabilities can have wide ranging societal impacts and care must be taken to ensure these impacts are positive. For example, improved modeling capabilities can help design better enzymes and drug candidates that can then go on to improve the lives of many people. Conversely, these general purpose techniques could also be misused to design toxic substances. To mitigate these risks, we do not present any specific methods to apply Multiflow to tasks that could be easily adjusted to the design of harmful substances without expert knowledge.

query_input.scenario: Protein sequence generation for therapeutic design
query_input.issue: Generated sequences may contain subtle deviations from biophysical or functional constraints
hazard: Discrete flow models can generate data with distributional biases or errors that are hard to detect because flow-matching objective does not guarantee exact distribution matching.
exposure: Biomedical researchers designing protein sequences using flow-based generative models

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Biomedical researchers lack means to detect subtle distributional biases in flow-model outputs, relying on generated sequences without exact distribution guarantees.
hipporag_result.impact: Non-viable or hazardous protein therapeutics advance through development, wasting resources and endangering patient safety.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Discrete flow models’ hard-to-detect distributional biases, through biomedical researchers designing protein sequences with them, trigger reliance on outputs without exact distribution guarantees, leading to non-viable or hazardous therapeutics advancing, wasting resources and endangering patient safety.

dose_response: Undetectable distributional biases in flow-generated sequences enable subtly flawed therapeutic candidates, which in protein design scenarios bypass expert scrutiny, leading to advancement of non-viable candidates into costly preclinical testing.
vulnerability: Over-reliance on generative outputs without orthogonal experimental validation due to time and cost pressures in drug development pipelines.
Lack of clinician and researcher training to interpret model uncertainty, leading to misplaced trust in plausible-looking sequences.
impact: Advancement of non-viable therapeutic candidates into costly preclinical testing, wasting resources and delaying effective treatments.
Erosion of trust in AI-assisted drug discovery among regulators and the biomedical community after high-profile failures.
